item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Absolutely love this one. Really simple concept but you can definitely imagine the grief and guilt the team would have felt upon realising one of these random disassociated objects was their colleague.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: I don't know how I'd never read this, since the author is probably in my personal top 3 favorites on this site… this is just absolutely, sublimely perfect.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: … I just realized something. This thing's effect turns everyone into a very selective, insistant case of the man who mistook his wife for a hat!

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: I love scips that screw with my head and leave me reading comments just to be able to understand. Upvoted.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: No, actually, no one did.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
For being 'safe' this thing is existentially fucking terrifying.

And imagine if something Keter fell into it …

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: More of a side effect. It was well-described earlier. It's like someone shifted the tags on things (people, washer, lamp, pencil). So when you look at the wallet, you see "Jim." Yet when you look at the dead body, you see a washer/dryer unit. Yet at some level you realize these things are confused. Cognitive Dissonance does the rest.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: is the confusion part of the anomalous effects?

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: I was set to no-vote in confusion until I read the discussion and realized that this was the reaction I was supposed to have. I love shit like this. Take my upvote, you brilliant bastard.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Every once and a while as I browse back through older SCPs I come across one that I find so mind-bogglingly strange or scary or just plain clever that I can't fathom a person not finding it brilliant. This is one of those SCPs.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Any relation to the fact that everything is bees?

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: What if the wabbajack was a room, and we were all cheese? sheogorath is pleased

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
Love it! +1
I wonder what it says about me that I read this and SCP11-93 back to back, and liked both, but felt them insightful, but unstrange. They serve to underscore the possibility that we are poorly equipped/positioned to perceive "reality" as it "is," and that if we cannot perceive it accurately, we cannot properly conceive of how we are inaccurately perceiving it…

I love your philosophical SCPs. Thanks.

This post brought to you be that wordy introspection that comes at 2:30 in the morning…

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: What I like in this SCP, apart from the masterfully done mindscrew, is that anything subjected to it could very well be classified as a SCP ("It's a sentient wallet that walks around and speaks as if it was human"). Maybe some SCPs around are simply normal objects or people that have suffered this identity switch…

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: ophite, once again demonstrating the greatest of mind screws. Bravo!

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: This is almost impossible to write correctly, but you did it.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: I have to say I was ready to downvote when I didn't get anything. But after reading the SCP after skimming the comments, I realise this is incredibly clever. Bravo, Ophite! +1

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Yeah. I had to go over that very, very, very carefully to make sure it came out right. The trick is the terminology — calling the lead in the pencil a 'filament' and the eraser 'insulation.'

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
Is it bad that when I read the thing about the light fixture, I didn't recognize it as anything BUT a light fixture?

+1, you even screwed with MY head.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
That sums up perfectly why I love this one, too.

Upvoted for sure.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
Oh, and here's something to consider.

When Jim starved to death, was that because we thought he was a washer/dryer… or because he thought he was a washer/dryer?

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: It's no worse than any other scientific mumbo-jumbo on this site. Like, say, every time I have to Google a scientific nomenclature just to find out "it's a fish".

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
I probably would have enjoyed this more if I knew what semiotics was before I read it. Now that I've done my homework, it's quite good. i particularly like the concrete/water.

But, there has to be a way to to describe the effect without jumping straight on a term that is slightly less commonly known than Jimmy Hoffa's burial site.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: This is very very very very good. Just my favorite kind of objects - it's not dangerous or scary, but it fucks with the way things are supposed to work so much that it transcends the fourth wall and now even readers are confused. Perfect.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: I almost never post here but I just had to say this is fantastic.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: I think this is one of my favorite SCPs so far: something that so fundamentally screws up everything around it that even the people "containing" it are scrambled to the point of being indecipherable. When i read that first description of the light fixture being cylindrical, yellow, and with a graphite filament, I sat my fiance down and forced her to listen to your entire article while I waved my arms like an emphatic mad man. Absolutely superb. +1

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: THIS is the kind of stuff I'm looking for. Holy crap. I'm glad someone thought semantics would be a good thing to screw with, because I can see this causing far more trouble than accidentally starving an agent to death because you thought he was a drier. And yes, I AM considering that a huge f'ing deal. Nice work.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Bingo.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: I love philosophical SCPs, if they exist.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: It occurs to me.. the water isn't actually water, is it? It's actually concrete. But because of the *properties* of the SCP, we just think it's water now, even though it has the same physical properties as concrete.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: This is painful in a good way, like hot peppers. I think it is well written, and supposed to be a bit hard to understand. Upvoted.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: This is so goofy, and yet conceptionally brilliant, I absolutely must like it. The only part I didn't get was SCP-1539-04, the description was so vague aside from the fact that it was gray and used to make noise but now doesn't…. I couldn't figure it out. Jim's iPod maybe?

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
It's pretty much flat-out stated that there's some existential, cosmological level of identity that this area is fucking with. Each and every person that has ever looked at the wallet since it came out has been absolutely positive that it was Jim. Upon close inspection, looking over a number of factors of it, they could determine that it is in fact a wallet. Thus, Jim is a wallet.

Similarly, they all looked at a "small, cylindrical piece of yellow-painted wood with graphite in the middle" and instantly knew it was a light fixture. If you read that description of an object by itself, it would obviously be a pencil, but look at THAT PENCIL and it's obviously a light fixture.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Sorry. I took that as formally calling for deletion.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: I like this. It takes a while to wrap one's brain around it, but it's a damn clever idea. Upvote.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: The text doesn't detail whether a new person would look at Jim-wallet and say "Why are you guys being stupid with this wallet?", or whether the Platonic essence of Jim-ness has been transplanted into it and any new people that look at it will be "hey who's this unusually leathery dude". In that case, you'd need quite an extended philosophical exercise to solve the issue, like Drewbear said.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: He's not accusing you of anything malicious (at least, I don't think he is). He's just saying that this concept has been written up and posted before and was deleted, and that he didn't care for it then and doesn't care for it now. Bright tends to be somewhat terse in his replies.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
I think that's what's implied by the final section. There was a person/team that said that there was something wrong; that Jim couldn't really be a wallet, but the nature of the semiotic switching is such that people just dismissed it. It isn't until they try to treat "Jim" like they did when he was human did it finally, slowly penetrate that he wasn't reacting to their treatment like a human would. At which point they realized that, despite everything telling them that the wallet is "Jim", their cognition regarding it is wrong. But it took such a long time and so much effort to come to that conclusion, and they still mentally label the wallet as Jim even knowing that it can't really be, that they're not sure which of the other objects actually is him.

In short:
Jim is a wallet. Jim is human. Humans aren't wallets. [BRAINLOCK] …this maybe isn't Jim? But it looks just like him! Oh, that rotting corpse? That's obviously the washer/dryer combo, duh.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: And that's why it gets my +1.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: This is definitely on of the better SCPs I've read, but maybe it's because I'm a huge literary theory nerd, and this just made me think of some serious sign, signifier, signed shit.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
I can assure you: this has never been up on the wiki. Check my publication history.

You didn't like it when I workshopped it. You continue not to like it. That's fine. But I haven't done anything even slightly questionable in deciding to actually publish it.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: This has been deleted before. I'd like to see it deleted again.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
I think this might share a common origin with me the goddamn TOASTER!

I do like the statement that this is a "conceptual disassociation zone", though - like a temporal anomaly, only related to epistemology rather than time.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
It took me a second read through, but it's so god damn bizarre and mind warping that I have to like it.

It's not a deal breaker for me, but do you know about the footnote tags1? It might be a better fit for that note.

Footnotes
1. Like this one for instance

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Yeah. That's the Site 17 intern or something double-checking containment protocols.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
I'm going to read through this again, but I'm just double checking

[Mistake? No such substance catalogued. — Ed.]

that is indeed supposed to be there correct? It's an independent auditor of containment files making a note?

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
Sorry, I should say I understand the writing and concept. The other's posts have helped clarify what I thought of it. Just some phrasing and words used took a little while to wrap my mind around (others have already mentioned some examples).

It is still a great entry though, especially that of the washing machine.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: It's long and drawn out. I got it, but it took me a second read-through.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: I am … not sure there's anyone that's not a layman in this. This is a type of thing that doesn't exist, isn't purported to exist, and … well. What are you having problems with?

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: The concept is fantastic (thanks to everyone here for the clarification), but the writing needs work for us laymen.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
Agent Jim Thayer is a brown leather wallet

They notice he's a wallet but they somehow don't notice that a wallet isn't a person? I get that this thing switches identities but wouldn't an outside observer go "wait, what the fuck are we doing?"

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: An utter mindfuck that made me think about philosophy and is scary on a fundamental existential level. Very cool, +1.

item.title: Re: Post unrelated
item.content:encoded: No idea.

item.title: Re: Post unrelated
item.content:encoded:
[[collapsible show="HOT" hide="DOCLOMGS"]]
(_)(_)lllllllllllD~~~
[[/collapsible]]

gives

HOT
DOCLOMGS

(_)(_)lllllllllllD~~~

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Test log? Did someone say test log?

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: I actually like it. It's a good mindfuck, and the way people talk about a wallet as if it's a person is unsettling. Good work.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
Alright, here's my view of it for people that are really having trouble on the uptake:

+ show block
– hide block

The basement switches the "identities" of two things. An object A with object B's identity will be treated as object B, even though it may look like a rather unusual instance of object B.
SCP-1539-01 is a pencil that has the identity of a light fixture.
SCP-1539-02 is a dead body that has the identity of a washer/dryer set.
SCP-1539-03 is a wallet that has the identity of Jim Thayer.
SCP-1539-04 is possibly a phone that lost its identity altogether.

The Foundation has been trying to treat a wallet back to health while poor Jim rotted, and even now they can't tell who is who.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
I like the basic idea, but I think some of the jargon should be broken down into simpler terms to make it a bit easier to identify what the SCP is. "SCP-1539-00 is an irregularly-shaped region of space, with a maximum length of 6.3 m." to open with, instead of "SCP-1539-00 is a semantic dissociation zone of irregular shape." I know what a region of space is; a semantic dissociation zone, on the other hand, is a harder concept to grasp: I recognize all the words individually, but the three in combination are difficult to parse.

+1 regardless, on the theory that I may be the only reader struggling with the jargon.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: You know, something's reminding me of that tale about a scratched gramophone record.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: I was gonna ask, did somebody at some point store a toaster in there?
